It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - A black maze structure with white passages and red paths - Blue regions appear to be player/agent positions (one inside the maze at rows 8-12, one at bottom-right) - Red (R) forms a path running down the right wall and across the bottom - Top-left blue square with white center may be a legend/indicator - Goal likely involves navigating through the maze, possibly following the red path
- Goal model: Navigate the blue player through the maze, likely following the red path to the goal
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT move the player; SPACE purpose unknown
- Plan: First probe with a single move to understand player movement, then solve the maze
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing SPACE:
1. The player (node 6) boundary changed dramatically - it now shows a huge boundary that seems to cover the entire maze structure. This is strange.
2. The black dot (node 8) boundary also changed to match the red path's shape.
3. Looking at the ASCII, the player is now at row 8, cols 13-18 (the leftmost position). The player moved from cols 20-24 to cols 13-18.
Wait, looking more carefully at the ASCII:
Row 8: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
The b's are at cols 14-19 (within the 13-49 range, so cols 14-19 in the full grid).
So SPACE seems to have moved the player back to the leftmost position (cols 13-18 approximately). This might be a "reset" or "teleport to start" action.
But wait - the player boundary shows a huge shape. Let me re-examine. The boundary for node 6 is:
[[7, 13], [7, 43], [13, 43], [13, 42], [14, 41], [41, 41], [41, 20], [42, 19], [48, 19], [49, 20], [49, 41], [48, 42], [48, 50], [56, 50], [56, 42], [55, 41], [55, 13]]
This is a very large boundary covering most of the maze. This suggests that the blue color (b) now appears in many places on the board, not just the player. Looking at the ASCII again:
Row 8: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBWWWWWW
Row 9: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 10: BbbBbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 11: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRRRBBWWWWWW
Row 12: BbbbbbBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBBRBBBWWWWWW
The b's are at cols 14-19 in rows 8-12. But the boundary suggests blue extends much further. Let me check the full board.
Actually, looking at the image, the player is now at the TOP-LEFT of the maze (blue square at top-left corner). The top-left indicator also changed from blue to gray.
So SPACE seems to:
1. Move the player to the top-left starting position
2. Change the top-left indicator color
This might be a "start" or "reset" action. Let me check if the game is now in a different state where movement works differently.
Let me try moving RIGHT again from this new position.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (updated):
- SPACE moved player to top-left position (cols ~14-18, rows 8-12)
- Player boundary expanded massively - blue now appears throughout the maze
- Top-left indicator changed from blue to gray
- SPACE may be a "reset" or "activate" action that changes the game state

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check the full board state after SPACE
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes'][:20]:
    print(f"  id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:3]}...")

# Check what the player (node 6) looks like now
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 6:
        print(f"\nPlayer (id=6): color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
        print(f"  boundary: {n['boundary']}")

# Let me check the top-left corner
ascii_grid = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nTop-left corner (rows 0-7, cols 0-12):")
for i in range(0, 8):
    row = ascii_grid[i]
    print(f"Row {i:2d}: {row[:12]}")

# Try RIGHT from new position
result = action(['RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] == 6:
        print(f"\nAfter RIGHT: player pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}...")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
